# Claims: Gamble, *The Palaeolithic Societies of Europe* (1999) — Range 1
**Source:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
**Slice file:** `/home/mark/Documents/Social Sciences/scratchpad/gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe/cache/range_1_850_3910.txt`
**Full-book line range:** 850–3910
**Coverage:** COMPLETE — entire cache slice read (file lines 1–~3061). Content: mid/late Ch.1 “Pulling aside the Palaeolithic curtain”; full Ch.2 “The individual, society and networks”; opening of Ch.3 “A Palaeolithic framework: locales, rhythms and regions” (through settlement-model setup). Slice begins mid-Ch.1 (post-opening on Childe/research status), not at book p.1.
**Claim ID prefix:** R1-
**Extractor notes:** Quotes from this slice only; attribution Gamble 1999 or named secondary he cites. OCR page-headers/hyphenation cleaned in quotes for readability. Wilson citations flagged as entity mismatches (not Godfrey Wilson).

---

## Chapter 1 — Pulling aside the Palaeolithic curtain

### Claim R1-001: Lower/Middle Palaeolithic research status since Childe 1951
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[palaeolithic]], [[childe-v-gordon]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble argues that since Childe’s 1951 remarks, the Lower and Middle Palaeolithic have achieved the research status Childe then gave the Upper Palaeolithic and Mesolithic, via large multi-disciplinary projects, continuous Pleistocene deep-sea isotopic chronology (Imbrie and Imbrie 1979), and science-based dating beyond radiocarbon (Aitken 1990).
- **Quote:** "What has happened since Childe’s remarks in 1951 is that the Lower and Middle Palaeolithic have now achieved the research status that he gave then to the Upper Palaeolithic and Mesolithic."
- **Notes:** Opening of this slice continues Ch.1’s curtain argument; frameworks now “encourage interpretation rather than mere description.”

### Claim R1-002: Artists’ reconstructions as theoretical arguments
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[bilzingsleben]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Citing Moser (1992), Gamble holds that Palaeolithic artists’ reconstructions are not neutral summaries but “reinforcements of theories” and “arguments in themselves,” implicitly directing interpretation (e.g. Bilzingsleben bison attack; Lazaret tent; Torralba/Ambrona elephant hunt).
- **Quote:** "these images ‘are more than just illustrations which summarize information presented in the text; rather they are reinforcements of … theories, and as such constitute arguments in themselves’ (1992:833)."
- **Notes:** Sites named as image-examples: Bilzingsleben, Lazaret, Torralba/Ambrona.

### Claim R1-003: Natural-history images close the curtain on Palaeolithic society
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble’s position is that naturalistic deep-time reconstructions control theoretical imagination and make a natural-history approach comfortable, thereby drawing a curtain across Palaeolithic society as an investigative object.
- **Quote:** "But while these images appear to open a window into the Palaeolithic, they also close curtains. The most important curtain they draw across our investigations is the subject of this book, Palaeolithic society."
- **Notes:** Cites Stoczkowski, Gamble, Moser, Gifford-Gonzalez, Rudwick.

### Claim R1-004: Palaeolithic never a seed-bed for social theory
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]], [[binford-lewis]], [[bordes-francois]], [[binford-bordes-mousterian-debate]], [[processual-archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble asserts the Palaeolithic has never been a seed-bed for social theory in archaeology; major debates instead concerned formation of the record and lithic/faunal variability (Binford; Bordes; Dibble; Mellars; Stiner and Kuhn), with middle-range/taphonomic work clarifying what is knowable without explaining patterns as social.
- **Quote:** "The Palaeolithic has never been a seed-bed for the discussion of social theory within archaeology."
- **Notes:** Explicitly links Binfords’ critique of Bordes’s five Mousterian “tribes” as a move away from culture history and social explanation.

### Claim R1-005: Ecological natural prehistory vs social prehistory
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[mithen-steven]], [[palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble argues that even when evolutionary ecology (optimality, decision rules, risk avoidance — Bettinger, Mithen, Torrence) expands the discussion, emphasis remains on natural rather than social prehistory.
- **Quote:** "the emphasis is firmly on the side of a natural rather than a social prehistory."
- **Notes:** Contrast with later social-archaeology literature he lists (Flannery, Friedman & Rowlands, Renfrew; then Barrett, Hodder, Shanks & Tilley, etc.).

### Claim R1-006: Interpretative curtain and civilizing-process narrative
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]], [[post-processual-archaeology]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[neanderthals]], [[modern-human-origins-debate]]
- **Claim:** Gamble holds that Palaeolithic explanation has stressed oppositions (ancient/modern humans; environment/culture; foraging/agriculture) that underwrite a civilizing-process narrative; an “interpretative curtain” has divided Palaeolithic from later archaeology and largely excluded post-processual engagement—though the curtain has been pulled for Mesolithic and Upper Palaeolithic and now falls across Middle–Upper / Neanderthal–Modern transition, merely shifted ~30,000 years.
- **Quote:** "the curtain which separates Us from the Other, Civilized from Uncivilized, now falls across the Middle to Upper, Neanderthal to Modern, transition… All we have apparently done is to shift its position by some 30,000 years."
- **Notes:** Cites Stringer and Gamble 1993; Gamble wants the curtain scrapped altogether.

### Claim R1-007: Society as unifying focus — four convictions
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[archaeology]], [[anthropology]], [[palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble’s solution is society as unifying focus, grounded in four convictions: (1) no different explanatory standards on either side of the curtain (ecological vs social switch unjustified); (2) archaeology as anthropology of the past must enlarge the variety of societies studied, exploring alternatives to ethnography (Wobst 1978); (3) social life is central to explaining stasis and change, against asocial population/environment determinism (critiques of Bender, Lourandos; vs Johnson and Earle); (4) archaeology must claim a place in the social as well as historical sciences.
- **Quote:** "My solution is to take society as a unifying focus… My interest is in the differences among Palaeolithic societies and not the divisions between them."
- **Notes:** Active individual engagement with environment stressed over passive adaptation.

### Claim R1-008: No palaeosociology of the Palaeolithic
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble adopts network-analysis vocabulary to translate evidence across timescales but explicitly refuses a “palaeosociology” (or palaeopsychology/palaeoanthropology) of the period; archaeological accounts will not simply vindicate one modern social theory over another.
- **Quote:** "I will not be arguing for a palaeosociology of the Palaeolithic any more than for a palaeopsychology or palaeoanthropology of the period."
- **Notes:** Still aims to influence future social-theory syntheses by broadening human social experience beyond industrialization/civilization.

### Claim R1-009: Facts meaningful within stories; verification retained
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[ingold-tim]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Against pure objectivism (e.g. Flannery and Marcus 1994 on science vs social agenda), Gamble holds Palaeolithic archaeology cannot be separated from social/political agendas; following Haraway, facts are meaningful within stories, with Ingold’s caveat that stories are not therefore unreal—while retaining scientific concern for verifying narratives.
- **Quote:** "facts are only meaningful when contained within stories (1989)."
- **Notes:** Few existing Palaeolithic “stories” are mostly origins/change narratives (Conkey & Williams, Knight, Landau), not long stasis.

### Claim R1-010: Social archaeology’s basic assumptions not given in Palaeolithic
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble argues Palaeolithic societies are not waiting to be revealed by attribute checklists; basic social-life elements assumed in much social archaeology (language, material symbolism, reflexive thought, kinship metaphor, ritual, differential power) cannot be taken for granted for this period.
- **Quote:** "the basic assumptions of the elements of social life which underpin so much of social archaeology cannot be taken for granted."
- **Notes:** From such elements one can mix many social theories without mentioning subsistence, ecology, selection, demography.

### Claim R1-011: Washburn 1961 *Social life of early man* as multi-disciplinary template
- **Register:** FACT
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[anthropology]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble treats Washburn’s 1961 Wenner-Gren synthesis *Social life of early man* as a “great” multi-disciplinary synthesis whose importance rests on (1) multi-disciplinary natural-science-heavy approach to human origins; (2) Washburn and DeVore’s baboon–forager comparison as standard thematic framework; (3) retrospectively, encouraging Lee’s !Kung work and the path to *Man the hunter*.
- **Quote:** "The importance of Social life of early man rests on three factors."
- **Notes:** Gamble notes the volume says little about social organization beyond event-timetables (cannibalism, fire) and speculation.

### Claim R1-012: Nomadic-style basic model of hunters and gatherers
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[patrilocal-band]], [[composite-hunting-band]]
- **Claim:** Gamble reports Lee and DeVore’s (1968) comparative “nomadic-style” model distinguishing organizational base (open systems, home base, male hunt/female gather division, sharing) from social system (mobility, small groups, alliances, rare exclusive rights, fission for conflict)—offering an alternative to Service’s patrilocal land-holding band model.
- **Quote:** "this basic model expanded on the views summarized by Washburn and DeVore… The emphasis on open systems, alliances, mobility and lack of territory… did offer an alternative view to the patrilocal, land-holding model of bands promoted by Service."
- **Notes:** Table 1.1 summarized in text.

### Claim R1-013: Stanner estate–range continuum under ecological conditions
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[patrilocal-band]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (via Stanner/Peterson) reports that open vs closed foraging organization is a continuum: when estate and range sizes converge under rich resources, restricted/patrilocal systems are more likely; when range far exceeds estate under poorer resources, open systems with sharing result.
- **Quote:** "The closer in size that a group’s estate and range become, the more likely it is to find a patrilocal, restricted access system. On the contrary, when resources are poorer, range size will far exceed the area covered by a group’s estate… unrestricted access, sharing and an open rather than closed system."
- **Notes:** Table 1.2 Australian gradient (Pintupi→Yolngu/Kurnai); inclusion vs exclusion principles.

### Claim R1-014: Dichotomies that characterize hunters and gatherers
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[binford-lewis]], [[patrilocal-band]], [[composite-hunting-band]]
- **Claim:** Gamble tabulates recurrent dichotomies used to characterize past and present foragers (patrilocal vs composite; closed vs open; collector vs forager; delayed vs immediate return; complex vs simple; curated vs expedient, etc.), warning that extremes often become definitional for archaeological analysis despite continuum glosses.
- **Quote:** "this gloss is often forgotten, and the extremes are used as definitions that structure archaeological analysis and characterize the prehistoric hunter-gatherer way of life."
- **Notes:** Table 1.3 includes Binford 1980 collector/forager; Woodburn delayed/immediate; Service/Steward band types.

### Claim R1-015: Human-origins research: social as multi-disciplinary paradigm
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[archaeology]], [[anthropology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble argues that in African multi-disciplinary human-origins research the social functioned less as thick analysis than as a paradigm linking disparate data into coherent narratives of change and adaptation (Isaac’s home bases, networks, protocultural superstructure on great-ape base).
- **Quote:** "the social, in the context of research into human origins, was a way to link disparate, multi-disciplinary data together to form a coherent narrative. It was the paradigm which justified the enterprise."
- **Notes:** Lists many model-builders (Reynolds, Isaac, Foley, Knight, Quiatt & Reynolds, etc.).

### Claim R1-016: Latour and Strum — more facts, less coherent framework
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble endorses Latour and Strum’s (1986) finding that science-based multi-disciplinary origins accounts (e.g. Leakey and Lewin *Origins*) can be less coherent structurally than Rousseau, yielding an inverse law: more incorporated facts, less attention to framework coherence.
- **Quote:** "there appears to be an inverse law at work in our examples: the more facts exist and are incorporated, the less attention is paid to the coherence of the framework within which those facts are placed."
- **Notes:** Facts need narrative balance; social-origins enterprise not dismissed, but assumptions must be explicit.

### Claim R1-017: Service band typology and composite vs patrilocal bands
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[patrilocal-band]], [[composite-hunting-band]], [[kuper-adam]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble recounts Service’s typology: many ethnographic “composite” bands treated as contact-destroyed remnants of former patrilocal bands (exogamy + virilocal residence), which Service claimed were the widespread Palaeolithic form; Lee challenged patrilocal bands as ethnographically rare, favoring bilateral fission–fusion open groups (!Kung).
- **Quote:** "These, according to Service, would have been the widespread form of social organization during the Palaeolithic… Lee, on the other hand, challenged the widespread nature of patrilocal bands, regarding them as ethnographically rare."
- **Notes:** Kuper 1988 on slow recognition of hunter-gatherer social life; composite bands (Algonkians, Athabaskans) as fluid family-level organization after contact.

### Claim R1-018: Encapsulated vs unencapsulated; delayed vs immediate return
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[composite-hunting-band]]
- **Claim:** Gamble reports Woodburn’s (1991) division of contemporary foragers into encapsulated immediate-return (Africa, India, Malaysia examples) vs unencapsulated delayed-return (North America, Australia), with revisionist critique (Schrire, Wilmsen) tying “open” forms to wider political economy; Woodburn treats delayed-return systems as pre-adapted for agriculture/pastoralism.
- **Quote:** "delayed-return hunter-gatherer systems are, in a sense, pre-adapted for the development of agriculture and pastoralism."
- **Notes:** Australia troubles the scheme; Woodburn treats Australian marriage as delayed return—“farmers in disguise.”

### Claim R1-019: Property and space vs system of return
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[locale]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (via Layton 1986) criticizes exclusive focus on delayed/immediate return: transactions in property (Australian sacred sites/estates; exclusive territorial rights elsewhere) shape long-term political structure; property and spatial use of resources focus social action through rights and obligations.
- **Quote:** "Property, the spatial use of resources, provides the focus for social action through involvement based on rights and obligations."
- **Notes:** Also Meillassoux on land as subject not instrument of labour and difficulty of hunter→farmer transition.

### Claim R1-020: Critique of population growth as primary determinant
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]]
- **Claim:** Gamble rejects Johnson and Earle’s (1987) scheme fitting Olduvai-to-Middle Palaeolithic foragers to !Kung/Shoshone parameters and attributing Upper Palaeolithic change primarily to continued population growth; open/composite systems treated as ancestral and foragers as passive—an unsatisfactory evolutionism that has not absorbed Wobst’s “ethnography with a shovel” lesson.
- **Quote:** "Everything that happens in the Upper Palaeolithic was ‘impelled by a continued growth in population’… population growth is the primary determinant of all social change… This last account of social change is clearly unsatisfactory."
- **Notes:** Active social agents vs “cold” societies pushed from outside (Bender 1985).

### Claim R1-021: Band Society as closed mating networks and boundaries
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[patrilocal-band]], [[gamble-clive]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]]
- **Claim:** Gamble distinguishes ordinary bands from “Band Society” (Wobst): underlying cultural system featuring group closure and boundaries, relying on Birdsell demography/ecology for Australian dialect tribes; applied by archaeologists to Upper Palaeolithic major change; Williams’s six postulates (territoriality, patrilocal tendency, kinship units, lineage band, exogamy, optimal band size for marriage alliances).
- **Quote:** "The essential feature of Band Society is group closure and the appearance of boundaries."
- **Notes:** Separate also from “complex hunter-gatherers”; Band Society almost certainly necessary for material complexity, per Gamble’s archaeological prediction.

### Claim R1-022: Wobst mating network, style zones, open-to-closed trajectory
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[behavioral-modernity]]
- **Claim:** Gamble reports Wobst’s mating-network/connubium model: closed networks enable archaeological analysis of regional process via stylistic transmission; closure raises social expense and lowers reproductive success; stylistic ethnic-boundary elements rare until mid-last glaciation, so Band Society/closed mating may not extend to early *Homo sapiens*—historical sequence open→closed.
- **Quote:** "Stylistic elements that may be taken to symbolize boundary processes between ethnic groups and social group affiliation are altogether rare up to the middle of the last glaciation… Band Society… may not even extend back to early Homo sapiens."
- **Notes:** Gilman 1984 figures linking co-operative hunting/herds and technology to closed connubia and style zones.

### Claim R1-023: Gilman vs Wobst; alliance networks beyond mating
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[kuper-adam]], [[agency]]
- **Claim:** Gamble endorses Gilman’s critique that Wobst equates the social with the demographic (mating network ≠ society), but also weakens Lévi-Strauss-style exogamy-as-paramount group integration: alliances include non-kinship partnerships (age, sex, affective/material/symbolic ties); negotiated individual alliance networks are larger analytic units than group-based connubia, with open or closed boundaries at regional scales.
- **Quote:** "The alliance network, negotiated by individuals, rather than the mating network with its emphasis on groups, provides a more comprehensive, larger unit for the analysis of Palaeolithic society."
- **Notes:** Gamble 1982/1986a cited; Band Society as one expression of alliance networks.

### Claim R1-024: Complex foragers checklist and critique of complexity class
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[behavioral-modernity]]
- **Claim:** Gamble reports Price and Brown’s complexity checklist (density, settlement size, permanent shelter/ceremony, art, burial differentiation) appearing from ~35,000 BP Upper Palaeolithic; he argues the classification is largely redundant if many/all foragers have some items—complexity can be assumed for Upper Palaeolithic *and* earlier societies; what matters is leading complicated social lives; economic/cultural complexity resides in hunter-gatherer social life and negotiation, not checklist adaptation alone.
- **Quote:** "I would rephrase it to state that economic and cultural complexity resides in hunter-gatherer social life."
- **Notes:** Brown and Price bury the band as evolutionary-typology unit; three themes: intensification, sedentism, inequality.

### Claim R1-025: Culture as unhelpful nature:culture curtain for Palaeolithic
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]], [[exaptation]]
- **Claim:** Gamble argues archaeological “culture” as extrasomatic adaptive rules/symbols set against Nature drops the interpretative curtain (when did we become cultural?), and cannot explain culture; primatological continuum views culture as regulating social information under selection—adaptive and exaptive—but culture has no life of its own and springs from active engagement of people living and interacting.
- **Quote:** "The concept of culture is therefore unhelpful to the study of the Palaeolithic unless the main aim is to examine our own definitions… culture cannot be used to explain culture."
- **Notes:** Cites Gould & Vrba exaptation; culture varies because individuals selectively call on different resources for relationships (Ch.2).

### Claim R1-026: Knight’s sex-strike culture narrative rejected
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[behavioral-modernity]]
- **Claim:** Gamble summarizes Knight’s (1991) narrative—female collective sex strike, menstrual synchrony, meat-for-sex, blood symbolism; Upper Palaeolithic “revolution” as ecological decline breaking female solidarity and male appropriation of power symbolism—and rejects it in essence and detail as environmentally/sociobiologically deterministic, while acknowledging it as a compelling story useful as negative model.
- **Quote:** "While I disagree with it in both essence and detail, I nonetheless acknowledge that it is a compelling account which studies of Palaeolithic society need, if only to show us that this is not what we want."
- **Notes:** Narrative example of culture-origins storytelling.

### Claim R1-027: Leroi-Gourhan *Gesture and speech* — techniques as social; chaînes opératoires
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[gamble-clive]], [[agency]]
- **Claim:** Gamble prefers Leroi-Gourhan’s *Le geste et la parole* / *Gesture and speech*: techniques are social productions (via Mauss); fusing technical and social process studies long-term social change through evolution of techniques without treating culture as a freestanding concept; dissolves style/function split; body, rhythms, and operational sequences (*chaînes opératoires*) produce forms/traditions; externalization of memory reorganizes action; graphism in Upper Palaeolithic is a revolution of rhythmic systems but the approach’s value is reuniting Palaeolithic with social archaeology/history.
- **Quote:** "by uniting technical and social processes, Leroi-Gourhan proposed a single approach to the study of the Palaeolithic… Society does not just reside in the former as rules about identity and ethnicity but as social representations literally embodied in the acts of manufacture, use and discard."
- **Notes:** Gamble: narrative “deserves to succeed” despite progressive-change faith.

### Claim R1-028: Two uses of the social in Palaeolithic studies
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[modern-human-origins-debate]], [[stringer-chris]]
- **Claim:** Gamble distinguishes (1) early African human-origins use of the social as multi-disciplinary research paradigm/deductive goal (proto-cultural platform) from (2) late-Palaeolithic use of the social as *explanation* (Band Society / complex society / social and cultural revolutions driving Upper Palaeolithic “human revolution”), leaving the long middle (*Homo erectus* dispersal to Upper Palaeolithic) mostly ecological/subsistence description.
- **Quote:** "Through its focus on social origins at either end of the Palaeolithic, this chapter has also highlighted how the period in between has received very little attention."
- **Notes:** Europe’s half-million-year record suited to change and stasis if “social” is better conceptualized.

---

## Chapter 2 — The individual, society and networks

### Claim R1-029: Social archaeology’s group-first checklist method
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[processual-archaeology]], [[gamble-clive]], [[agency]]
- **Claim:** Gamble argues social archaeology has concentrated on groups/institutions (cultures, systems, Service’s band–tribe–chief–state procession) and succeeds because categories are predefined—checklist comparison to polythetic prehistoric cultures (Clarke 1968)—while the individual is screened out as beyond Palaeolithic resolution (Clark) or crushed by “primitive society” institutional models (Kuper 1988).
- **Quote:** "archaeologists already know what they are going to find. Those chiefdoms and states… are already defined before the digging begins."
- **Notes:** Opens with Marx epigraph on making history under given circumstances.

### Claim R1-030: Mithen and Shanks & Tilley — individual cannot be screened out
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[mithen-steven]], [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[post-processual-archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble aligns with Mithen’s claim that good archaeological explanation requires reference to knowledgeable, planning individuals, and with Shanks and Tilley’s structuration-derived insistence that the individual is knowledgeable/active yet positioned in structured social fields—neither pure ciphers nor unconstrained free agents.
- **Quote:** "A good explanation in archaeology requires explicit reference to individual people going about their day to day business."
- **Notes:** Micro/macro (individual/group) never adequately addressed for Palaeolithic society.

### Claim R1-031: Top-down vs bottom-up models of society (Hinde)
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[anthropology]]
- **Claim:** Following Hinde (1976, 1987), Gamble contrasts top-down structural models (enduring institutions constrain individuals; society as architecture preceding members) with bottom-up models (individuals manage/map relationships; society performed into existence through interaction)—not mutually exclusive but he advocates shifting Palaeolithic analysis to bottom-up, bridged by network analysis.
- **Quote:** "In the former model the individual plays an active role in performing society and its structures into existence. In the latter, society precedes and shapes the individual through the power of its institutions."
- **Notes:** Also maps onto Ingold’s cognitivist vs phenomenological culture approaches (preview Ch.3).

### Claim R1-032: Top-down architecture and Durkheimian kinship institutions
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[anthropology]]
- **Claim:** In the architectural model, individuals enter pre-existing frameworks coded in culture; among “primitive societies” kinship institutions (clan, moiety, lineage, family) produce solidarity via collectivity of action, most closely associated with Durkheim—yielding individuals as demiurges serving institutional will, creativity largely denied.
- **Quote:** "These institutions produced solidarity which permitted social life through the operation of a collectivity of action, a widely held view most closely associated with Durkheim."
- **Notes:** Dominant model in social archaeology (Redman et al. 1978).

### Claim R1-033: Bottom-up performance — ethnographic creativity among foragers
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]]
- **Claim:** Gamble cites forager ethnography for performed/negotiated social life: Wilmsen on Zhu/!Kung kinship as dynamic keyboard; Wiessner hxaro trading partnerships linking large networks; Morris on Hill Pandaram creating social life generation by generation via dyadic bonds; Guemple on Belcher Island Inuit kinship as negotiable participation, not pure ascription; Bahuchet on Pygmy residence rule broken toward bilocal choice.
- **Quote:** "Kinship in Zhu [!Kung] society, rather than being a static straitjacket, is a dynamic keyboard on which individuals play variations on a theme of options."
- **Notes:** Wilson 1988 on kinship as reservoir not institution — entity flag (see R1-MISC-01).

### Claim R1-034: Primate societies: individual unit and performative turn
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[anthropology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble draws parallel methodological difficulty: primatologists observe behaviour and infer social life without self-declared kinship structures; current primate studies take the individual as unit of observation/analysis (Quiatt and Reynolds) and have shifted from architectural group metaphors to performance/negotiation models (Strum and Latour; Cheney and Seyfarth; Rowell; Hinde).
- **Quote:** "If we grant that baboons are not entering into a stable structure but rather negotiating what that structure will be… the variety of baboon society and its ill fit to a simple structure can be seen to be a result of the ‘performative’ question."
- **Notes:** Explicit analogy for Palaeolithic inference from static residues.

### Claim R1-035: Hinde three-tier model — interactions, relationships, structure
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble adopts Hinde’s three tiers—interactions → relationships → surface/social structure—with constraints across levels; face-to-face interactions basic; relationships as successions of interactions; sociality emergent (Foley and Lee); group structure as patterning of relationships; temporal patterning of relationships must be understood by animals.
- **Quote:** "Face-to-face interactions between individuals are identified as the basic elements of social structure, while relationships are defined as a succession of interactions between two individuals."
- **Notes:** Vervet alliances and female-core propinquity as primate examples.

### Claim R1-036: Structuration — duality of structure, agency for all hominids
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[giddens-anthony]], [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[neanderthals]], [[exaptation]]
- **Claim:** Gamble selects Giddens’s structuration: structure not external but contained in action; agency as capacity for action applies to Moderns, Neanderthals, and *Homo heidelbergensis*; duality of structure is both constraining and enabling (vs Durkheimian dualism of external constraint); language example—rules constrain yet enable creative performance; exaptation, not mere adaptation to speak.
- **Quote:** "Agency leads us back to the individual. It applies to Moderns, Neanderthals and Homo heidelbergensis since it is the capacity for action which is important in social life."
- **Notes:** Godelier quote: humans “produce society for living.” Resources and rules as dual elements fashioning social life.

### Claim R1-037: Social vs system integration; co-presence hierarchy
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[giddens-anthony]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble uses Giddens’s social integration (what people do together in co-presence — Goffman) vs system integration (patterns extended *in absentia*); Goffman’s hierarchy of social occasion → situation → gatherings (two+ individuals in immediate presence/co-presence); Palaeolithic difference turns on emotional, material, and symbolic resources brought to interactions and rules limiting network size/organization.
- **Quote:** "What marked the difference between Palaeolithic societies was… the resources — emotional, material and symbolic — which were brought to these interactions, and the rules — limits to network size and organization — which emerged from them."
- **Notes:** Scales from handaxe knapping minutes to Solutrean multi-millennial distributions.

### Claim R1-038: Two central evolutionary questions
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[release-from-proximity]], [[gamble-clive]], [[agency]]
- **Claim:** Gamble frames Palaeolithic-relevant social evolution around two questions: (1) what happens, socially, when you leave a gathering? (2) how did social life get so complicated?
- **Quote:** "what happens, socially, when you leave a gathering? and how did social life get so complicated?"
- **Notes:** Common issues: active individual actors; performance; personal interaction as negotiation context.

### Claim R1-039: Release from proximity
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[release-from-proximity]], [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[giddens-anthony]], [[gamble-clive]], [[behavioral-modernity]]
- **Claim:** Gamble identifies Giddens’s problem of stretching social relations across time/space beyond presence as central; primatologists independently stress uncoupling relationships from spatial proximity in humans (“release from proximity,” Rodseth et al. 1991) and “going beyond” available information in social cognition (Quiatt and Reynolds)—a major Palaeolithic question: when people went beyond rules set by immediate bodily resources.
- **Quote:** "Rodseth (et al. 1991:240) emphasize the importance of uncoupling relationships from spatial proximity in human rather than ape societies. They term this very aptly the ‘release from proximity’."
- **Notes:** Intense relations with artifacts, places, abstractions contrasted with early hominids.

### Claim R1-040: Complex vs complicated societies (Strum and Latour)
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[release-from-proximity]], [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[behavioral-modernity]]
- **Claim:** Strum and Latour (via Gamble): non-human primates live in *complex* societies built only with bodies, social skills, and strategies (limited stability); humans introduce *complication*—successions of simple operations using materials and symbols—which simplifies social tasks by holding factors constant, enabling specialists to effect time-space distanciation beyond co-presence limits.
- **Quote:** "Something is complicated… when it is made of a succession of simple operations… Simplification, by using symbols centred on people and objects, allows a variety of factors to be held constant in any social context."
- **Notes:** Answers how structure exists *in absentia*.

### Claim R1-041: Network analysis as form of analysis, not theory of action
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]], [[agency]]
- **Claim:** Gamble adopts network analysis to formalize interaction/negotiation and counter group-centred anthropology/sociology; stresses it is a form of analysis rather than a theory of social action (Boissevain, Mitchell); individuals are creative mobile nodes; society ultimately a “network of networks.”
- **Quote:** "it is a form of analysis rather than a theory of social action… Society ultimately becomes a network of networks."
- **Notes:** Bridges micro/macro; objects matter for archaeology as both evidence and structuring means.

### Claim R1-042: Multiplex vs uniplex ties
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble distinguishes multiplex ties (multiple strands—affinal, economic, affective—likely durable; e.g. sister/close friend) from uniplex ties (single-capacity links; e.g. greengrocer/bus-driver); quality of tie does not necessarily depend on interaction frequency.
- **Quote:** "Multiplex ties comprise several strands to a relationship forged between two individuals… By contrast, uniplex ties characteristically involve a single-capacity link in a relationship."
- **Notes:** Ties both “bind and bond.”

### Claim R1-043: Density, centrality, transitivity, reachability
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble defines network density (extent possible ties exist; high density ~ standardized values, low ~ individualism), centrality (accessibility; often higher where density lower), transitivity (A–B, B–C ⇒ A knows C; drives density and reachability), and reports small-world reachability of only 3–5 links to introduce a stranger in large populations.
- **Quote:** "In populations ranging from 300,000 people to 200 million, only 3-5 links are needed to reach some randomly selected person… and arrange for a personal introduction."
- **Notes:** Also graph structures (circle, chain, Y, wheel) and information efficiency (Leavitt/Argyle/Kapferer).

### Claim R1-044: Network structure and cultural transmission (Steele)
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble reports Steele’s (1994) simulations: weaker ties/lower connectivity (Poisson and Low Density lattices) spread new adaptive traits faster; high-density “Tribal” networks resist trait spread and enforce behavioural norms.
- **Quote:** "the weaker ties in his Poisson network and the lower connectivity in his Low Density network resulted in new adaptive traits spreading through the system faster… the very high density pattern of ties in his Tribal network produced its own internal resistance to the spread of new traits."
- **Notes:** Links structure to costs of location (Mandryk, Wobst).

### Claim R1-045: Age, sex, and division of labour structure networks
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble reports Wiessner hxaro partner counts peaking when adults seek spouses for children; Howell on !Kung women peaking connectedness earlier (shorter generation times, earlier marriage); Maryanski and Ishii-Kuntz (via Bott) link sex-specific network density to dispersal and role segregation; chimpanzee vs bonobo show closely related species with different sex-structured densities—social structure from history as much as genes.
- **Quote:** "the greater the density of the network of at least one sex, the greater the level of role segregation… So long as it does not affect reproductive success either pattern of sex-based alliance is possible."
- **Notes:** Implications for ancestral hominid pattern underdetermined; network-density implications remain.

### Claim R1-046: Three interlinked resources — emotional, material, symbolic
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]], [[release-from-proximity]]
- **Claim:** Following Turner and Maryanski, Gamble models network content via three interlinked resources—emotional, material/exchange, symbolic/stylistic—differentially used across intimate, effective, extended, and global networks (Table 2.8).
- **Quote:** "Turner and Maryanski (1991:550) have discussed the content of networks in terms of three interlinked resources: emotional, symbolic and material."
- **Notes:** Core of his Palaeolithic social toolkit; not a hunt for simple archaeological correlates but selective pressures on negotiation.

### Claim R1-047: Hierarchy of ego-based networks defined
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[giddens-anthony]], [[gamble-clive]], [[presence-and-co-presence]]
- **Claim:** Gamble defines: **Intimate** (~5) — high genetic/emotional investment, ontological security (Giddens), dense face-to-face; **Effective** (~20) — logistics of daily life, material bonds for reproductive/economic/political ends; **Extended** (100–400) — friends of friends, symbolic/style resources for negotiation; together = **Personal** network; **Global** — “otherness,” strangers/casual acquaintances, neutral for alliance, unfocused interaction in urban co-presence (Goffman).
- **Quote:** "Intimate network has a high investment of genetic and emotional resources… Effective network deals with the logistics of daily life… Extended network contains friends of friends… Global network is defined by ‘otherness’."
- **Notes:** Table 2.9; personal-network scale/content varies by individual.

### Claim R1-048: Resource use by network scale (reciprocity and style)
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Emotional resources dominate intimate networks; material resources important in intimate (generalized reciprocity, household sharing) and effective (marriage exchange, lineage/village), with balanced reciprocity characterizing extended/tribal sector and negative reciprocity global/intertribal “other”; symbolic/stylistic resources dedicate especially to effective and extended networks (Wobst target groups), while restricted code among intimates makes stylistic messaging unnecessary (Douglas).
- **Quote:** "Restricted code thrives upon face-to-face contact… The strong affective ties using emotional resources are therefore negatively correlated… with the presence of symbolically organized resources expressed stylistically."
- **Notes:** Same materials can shift gift↔commodity across network zones (Gregory, Valeri); Sahlins sectors diagram.

### Claim R1-049: Temporal rules — grooming time and language as vocal grooming
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]], [[release-from-proximity]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (via Dunbar) treats time as a constraint: grooming time rises with group size; ~20% of day is upper non-human primate grooming limit; human group ~150 would need ~42% time if only grooming—mitigated by fission–fusion and language as cheap multi-party “vocal grooming” (Aiello and Dunbar); neocortex limit applies to number of stable interpersonal relationships, not to kinship structuring specifically.
- **Quote:** "humans can use language as a grooming substitute… a ‘cheap’ form of social grooming because several people can be ‘groomed’ in the course of a single conversation."
- **Notes:** Temporal rules especially limit intimate/effective networks.

### Claim R1-050: Steele predictions of hominid group and home-range size
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[neanderthals]], [[gamble-clive]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]]
- **Claim:** Gamble reports Steele’s (1996) predictions from brain/body scaling: average group sizes e.g. *H. habilis* ~91, *H. erectus* ~108, early modern/Neanderthal ~309, *H. sapiens* ~317, with primate-model home-range diameters ~3–11 km vs much larger carnivore-model ranges.
- **Quote:** "Steele (1996) has extended this analysis and estimated hominid average group size (Table 2.10)."
- **Notes:** Archaeological range evidence deferred to later chapter; figures “as of” Steele 1996 via Gamble 1999.

### Claim R1-051: Cognitive rules — chunking, magic number, scalar stress
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble argues cognitive rules govern extended/global networks: short-term memory ~7 (Miller) or fewer items; chunking expands capacity; Johnson’s information-source costs and scalar stress—integrate ~5–7 chunks or introduce hierarchy; forager territories optimize ~6 neighbours; !Kung camps keep ~3 organizational chunks while population grows by re-chunking nuclear→extended families without formal hierarchy (Johnson from Yellen).
- **Quote:** "increasing difficulties in communication arise because… integration due to our own cognitive abilities breaks down unless Miller’s magic number of five to seven chunks is preserved."
- **Notes:** Kosse’s 500±100 face-to-face maximal unit revised: knowing 500 names ≠ knowing all relationships.

### Claim R1-052: Demographic sizes of intimate and effective networks
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Cross-cultural urban/network studies (Milardo): intimate mean ~5 (range ~3–7), only ~50% immediate kin—security/stability over pure kin selection; effective/exchange network mean ~20 (sample means 10–23; most individuals 6–34), kin ~40%; ethnographic parallels include Chicago gangs (Thrasher: many 6–20), Copper Eskimo pigatigiit seal-sharing partners (~14), Gwembe coalitions+ancestors (~18–27), !Kung ~16 relatives (Howell).
- **Quote:** "the number of people interacting at the highest intensity with ego ranged on average from three to seven persons, with an overall mean of five… the average size of this network… was twenty persons."
- **Notes:** Intimate multiplex high-density; effective less permanent, mix multiplex/uniplex.

### Claim R1-053: Extended and personal network size estimates
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Extended network estimates vary widely (Gurevich mean 400 over 100 days, range 72–1000+; Barth 109–175 in 14 days; Boissevain/Colson ~300); Bernard and Killworth hypothesize groups >~140 need subgroups/hierarchy; Dunbar links ~150 to language-bonded networks; Gamble’s summary: extended ~100–400; complete personal network ~130–430 (upper maybe 1000); only finite ties can be forged—new ties replace old.
- **Quote:** "a figure of between 100 and 400 people represents the best approximation we currently have of a person’s extended network… the complete personal network is of the order of 130 to 430, with an upper limit of possibly 1,000 people."
- **Notes:** Only ~25% overlap extended/effective (Milardo); variation supports individual-level selection material.

### Claim R1-054: Global network thresholds ~2000–2500
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]], [[patrilocal-band]]
- **Claim:** Global network is potentially unbounded “otherness” but at low densities Kosse’s ~2500 area-level threshold is supported by Wiessner’s ~2000 !Kung hxaro system, Constandse-Westermann and Newell’s mean ~2815 band-level linguistic families, and Bernard and Killworth’s ~2460 maximum stable group without formal hierarchy; beyond this, exclusion/identification violence narratives (Burch Alaska) define limits.
- **Quote:** "Kosse (1990:278) has identified a threshold of 2,500 which she regards as the upper limit on the area-level adaptation… supported by Wiessner’s estimate of 2,000 people in the !Kung hxaro trading system."
- **Notes:** Industrial unboundedness is relative; Gwembe example of knowing 600 names but intensive interaction with few.

### Claim R1-055: Exclusion and ontological security
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[giddens-anthony]], [[gamble-clive]], [[presence-and-co-presence]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (via Colson) argues humans’ distinctive capacity is exclusion so interaction intensity in the personal network stays constant; intimate/effective networks require heavy emotional-time investment for ontological security—trust that natural and social worlds are as they appear (Giddens)—producing “rounded characters” and demands met through reciprocity and visual information exchange.
- **Quote:** "What humans have developed… is our ability to exclude so that the intensity of interaction with those in our personal network is held constant."
- **Notes:** “People shock” of small communities vs urban anonymity; ritualized greetings as coping.

### Claim R1-056: Birdsell magic numbers recast as networks not institutions
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[patrilocal-band]], [[gamble-clive]], [[agency]]
- **Claim:** Gamble notes network sizes 5 / 20 / 100–400 match Birdsell’s hunter-gatherer “magic numbers” (nuclear family ~5; minimum band ~25, range 20–70; connubium 150–200; disputed dialect tribe/maximum band ~500) but rejects treating them as top-down demographic *institutions* of Palaeolithic society; recast as ego-centred networks for cultural transmission and cross-species comparison.
- **Quote:** "The point about these ‘magic numbers’ is that they have become the demographic institutions of Palaeolithic hunter-gatherer society… recasting these groups as networks, constructed by and centred on the individual… provides a more flexible approach."
- **Notes:** Explicit revision of group-based model.

### Claim R1-057: Ch.2 summary — bottom-up rules and resources for Palaeolithic
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[agency]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[giddens-anthony]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble summarizes: society’s double meaning (bounded system vs association) has rarely specifiable boundaries; prefer performative over architectural model because duality of structure enables and constrains; carry arguments into Palaeolithic by examining rules (temporal, cognitive) and resources (emotional, material, symbolic) that organize interaction and produce recurrent network sizes despite individual variation.
- **Quote:** "Society does not make us or vervet monkeys into social beings. First and foremost we are all social actors whose involvement with the world makes society the rich, individual experience, that it is for all of us."
- **Notes:** Sets up Ch.3 framework.

---

## Chapter 3 opening — Locales, rhythms and regions

### Claim R1-058: Two levels of analysis linked by rhythms of social technology
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[agency]], [[chaine-operatoire]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble argues investigating Palaeolithic *society* (not just settlement) requires two spatial levels—**locales** and **regions**—linked by **rhythms** of social technology (operational sequences, path movements, attention to others) that connect past dynamics to inert residues; rhythms are inferred from artifacts and embody agency.
- **Quote:** "investigating Palaeolithic society, rather than Palaeolithic settlement, involves two levels of analysis, locales and regions, which are linked by the rhythms of social technology."
- **Notes:** Methodological “piers” of the bridge between data and individuals/societies.

### Claim R1-059: Locales, landscape of habit, social landscape
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[ingold-tim]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Locales analyzed as archaeology of encounters, gatherings, social occasions, and places; regional scale supplemented by **landscape of habit** and **social landscape**; rhythms draw on Leroi-Gourhan and discussions of temporality/landscape (Gosden, Ingold, etc.) to supply vocabulary for European Palaeolithic social life, change, and stasis.
- **Quote:** "locales are discussed in terms of the archaeology of encounters, gatherings, social occasions and places… The regional scale is supplemented by two further concepts: the landscape of habit and the social landscape."
- **Notes:** Purpose: missing vocabulary for Chs. 4–7.

### Claim R1-060: Nine-region European settlement model
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]], [[global-colonization]]
- **Claim:** Building on Gamble 1986a’s spatial/demographic/social behavioural domains, he uses a nine-division regional model of Europe where latitude, longitude, and relief structure continentality, growing season, and resource productivity regardless of glacial/interglacial climate; Palaeolithic settlement varies across regions in density/frequency of occupation (not primarily artifact types), with long-term behavioural changes also tracked (art, points, knapping)—slice ends as this review continues.
- **Quote:** "at any one time Palaeolithic settlement varied across the nine regions of the continent, not in terms of artifact types, but rather in the density and frequency of occupation."
- **Notes:** End of assigned slice; full Ch.3 continues beyond line 3910.

---

## Cross-cutting / named debates and materials

### Claim R1-061: Binford–Bordes Mousterian as anti-social-explanation move
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[binford-bordes-mousterian-debate]], [[binford-lewis]], [[bordes-francois]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[processual-archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble frames the Binfords’ critique of Bordes’s five Mousterian tribes (lithic-assemblage cultures of SW France) as a deliberate move away from culture history and social explanation toward middle-range/taphonomic clarity about the record.
- **Quote:** "the Binfords’ critique of the five Mousterian tribes of southwest France… defined by their different lithic assemblages was a deliberate move away from culture history and social explanation."
- **Notes:** Context: patterns grew but were not explained as social factors.

### Claim R1-062: Upper Palaeolithic as social/stylistic revolution locus
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[behavioral-modernity]], [[modern-human-origins-debate]], [[neanderthals]]
- **Claim:** Across Ch.1, Gamble reports that Band Society, complex foragers, Conkey’s complex social geography, style zones, graphism, and “human revolution” literature concentrate major social explanation on the Upper Palaeolithic after ~35,000 BP, while he wants continuous social analysis without the Middle–Upper interpretative curtain.
- **Quote:** "Complexity… became a major factor in social life from 35,000 BP, the onset of the European Upper Palaeolithic."
- **Notes:** His book’s European half-million-year middle is the neglected object.

### Claim R1-063: Active individual engagement vs ecological determinism
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[processual-archaeology]], [[post-processual-archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble sides with critics of asocial intensification/population explanations, proposing active engagement and mutual involvement of the individual in constructing/negotiating environment—against passive “cold” foragers acted on by climate or civilization.
- **Quote:** "I will stress the active engagement and mutual involvement of the individual in the construction and negotiation of his/her environment."
- **Notes:** Bridges processual ecological data with social-theory vocabulary without full post-processual agenda adoption.

### Claim R1-064: Scientific method retained but boundaries of discussion expanded
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[archaeology]], [[giddens-anthony]]
- **Claim:** Gamble will not abandon scientific verification procedures for Palaeolithic societies; ecology/environment provide a mutual (not purely objective) standard for interpreting past action; aims to broaden scientific method toward common ground of historical and social sciences, expanding Palaeolithic discussion by taking society as focus.
- **Quote:** "In writing this book I have not found it possible, or desirable, to abandon the procedures of the scientific approach in dealing with the question of Palaeolithic societies."
- **Notes:** Notes Giddens’s near-rejection of science claims; Thomas 1991 on human action left out of narrow science.

## Miscellaneous (entity flags / mismatches)

### Claim R1-MISC-01: Wilson citations — not Godfrey Wilson
- **Register:** FACT
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Claim:** **ENTITY MISMATCH FLAG:** In this slice Gamble cites “Wilson (1980)” and “Wilson (1988)” in human-origins model lists and “Wilson observes (1988:36)” on kinship as reservoir not institution. These are not identifiable from the text as Godfrey Wilson; likely other Wilsons (e.g. E.O. Wilson / Peter J. Wilson 1988). Do not force [[wilson-godfrey]].
- **Quote:** "Kinship, as Wilson observes (1988:36), is not an institution in the way that it has normally been used to define primitive society."
- **Notes:** File under miscellaneous; curator to resolve bibliographically from Gamble’s references.

### Claim R1-MISC-02: Clark 1992a on individuals beyond Palaeolithic resolution
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[agency]], [[palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble cites “Clark” (1992a:107) arguing only cumulative group activities are preserved because individual actions exceed Palaeolithic resolution—a view Mithen counters. **Not** to be forced to a specific Clark thinker page without bibliography check (Grahame Clark vs others possible).
- **Quote:** "According to Clark, only the cumulative results of group activities are preserved because the actions of individuals are beyond the resolution of the Palaeolithic record (1992,a:107)."
- **Notes:** Mismatch risk if multiple Clarks in vault.

### Claim R1-065: Network approach facilitates cross-species comparison
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[neanderthals]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Recasting magic-number groups as resource-differentiated networks facilitates cross-species comparison of social behaviour and ties (Maryanski; Foley and Lee), differentiating elaboration by emotional/material/symbolic resources—even when ancestral patterning among *H. heidelbergensis*, Neanderthals, and *H. sapiens* cannot be uniquely specified.
- **Quote:** "the approach also facilitates cross-species comparison in social behaviour and network ties… where the relationships defined by emotional, material and symbolic resources differentiate the extent of network elaboration."
- **Notes:** Ties Ch.2 framework to later European case chapters.

### Claim R1-066: Artists’ consensus activities and gendered division of labour
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble notes reconstruction images create consensus about routine Palaeolithic activities—hunting, gathering, cooking, eating, child care, tool manufacture, hide scraping, sleeping, burying the dead—and which sexes/age groups were responsible (Gifford-Gonzalez, Moser).
- **Quote:** "Using these images allows us to reach consensus about what regularly took place — hunting, gathering, cooking, eating, child care, tool manufacture, hide scraping, sleeping, burying the dead — and which sexes and age groups were largely responsible."
- **Notes:** Natural-history comfort vs social portrayal difficulty (kinship charts vs naturalistic hunting scenes).

### Claim R1-067: Wobst 1978 — archaeology’s unique anthropological data range
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[anthropology]], [[archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble quotes Wobst (1978) that archaeologists uniquely command behavioural variance across personnel, space, and time scales—from individuals to continent-wide matrices and from events to millennia—justifying Palaeolithic contribution to social variety beyond ethnography.
- **Quote:** "Archaeologists are the only anthropologists whose data contain information about behavioural variance in all its dimensions."
- **Notes:** Under conviction 2 of the society-as-focus programme.

### Claim R1-068: No justification for switching explanation at agriculture or Upper Palaeolithic
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[upper-paleolithic-revolution]], [[palaeolithic]], [[post-processual-archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble’s first conviction: no reason for different procedures/standards for modern vs ancient humanity; no reason to switch from ecological to social explanation merely because the record changes with agriculture/sedentism or with a more varied Upper Palaeolithic record.
- **Quote:** "There is no reason to switch explanations from predominantly ecological to mainly social just because the nature of the archaeological record changes with the advent of agriculture and sedentism… Neither is there any reason to change explanations during the Palaeolithic as soon as a more varied record appears with the Upper Palaeolithic."
- **Notes:** Analysis over definitional division.

### Claim R1-069: Hxaro as negotiated effective/extended network example
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Wiessner’s hxaro partnerships, primarily among relatives but winding between camps into very large linked networks, exemplify choice and interaction in constructing systems; partner numbers vary strongly by age category (adolescents mean 10 → adults with mature children mean 24).
- **Quote:** "These are primarily formed between relatives but wind between camps with the result that very large networks of people become linked."
- **Notes:** Also anchors ~2000-person trading system as global-scale estimate.

### Claim R1-070: Internal OCR/page artifacts — no content duplication extracted twice
- **Register:** FACT
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Claim:** Slice contains running headers, page numbers, and occasional OCR noise (e.g. broken table characters, “a”/“¥” artifacts) but no substantial duplicated chapter-body passages requiring double extraction; claims above extract unique argumentative content once.
- **Quote:** n/a (meta)
- **Notes:** Flag for integrator: page-header lines are not claims.
